title: The Mutapa State
documentsInfo: We are using, in this text, references to Portuguese and the European calendar, as we are still studying the cultures being presented.

maps.text: Approximate location of the Mutapa state, in Zimbabwe and Mozambique.

common.text:
### This is mine… Are you sure?

Relying solely on what is written in texts can be problematic, as they may employ literary devices (<htcite0>) that are not understood by everyone. Faced with this scenario, we need evidence that allows us to confirm ideas. Fossils are an example of such evidence.

#### What is our origin?

When discussing our origin, it is not uncommon to see people thinking only of our own species, often due to a lack of knowledge about the true age of the planet or how species emerge.

Thanks to advances in knowledge, we now know that, although we are the only living species of the genus *Homo*, other species emerged before us, and their origin was precisely the African continent (<htcite1>). Among the species highlighted in this text is *Homo rudolfensis*, which lived between <htdate0> and <htdate1>, whose skull is shown in *Figure 2*:
Figure 2: Reconstruction of the Homo rudolfensis. © Don Hitchcock. Original at the Vienna Natural History Museum ().

#### Toward the south

The presence of *Homo* in southern Africa also dates back thousands of years. *Homo naledi* (**Figura 2**), por ejemplo, fue encontrado recientemente en Sudáfrica y habría vivido en la región entre <htdate0> y <htdate1> (<htcite0>).

Figure 3: The figure includes approximately all the material incorporated into this diagnosis, including the holotype specimen, paratypes, and referred material. These comprise 737 partial or complete anatomical elements, many of which consist of several reassembled specimens. Specimens not identified to the element level, such as non-diagnostic long bones or cranial fragments, and a subset of fragile specimens are not shown here. The arrangement of the ‘skeleton’ in the center of the photo is a composite of elements representing multiple individuals. This view is in perspective; the table on which the bones are arranged is 120 cm wide for scale reference. © Lee Roger Berger research team (). Shared under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike license.

#### *Homo* in Zimbabwe

The earliest evidence of the arrival of species carrying technology consists of worked stone fragments, developed using a technique known as the Levallois core (**Figure 4**). However, it is not possible to say with certainty that they were produced by humans like us (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

source.text: Brantingham, P. J., & Kuhn, S. L.

common.text: Figure 4: Levallois Core. © Musée du Louvre/ Département des Antiquités égyptiennes ().

Thus, we need more solid evidence to confirm the presence of *Homo sapiens* in this location.

#### Bantu People

Currently, most inhabitants of Zimbabwe speak the Shona language, which belongs to the Bantu group (<htcite0>).

The Bantu people originated in a region where Cameroon is located today, with archaeological evidence indicating settlement from at least <htdate0> (<htcite1>; <htcite2>; <htcite3>).

The arrival of Bantu peoples in the Zimbabwe region dates to between <htdate1> and <htdate2> (<htcite4>), and when they arrived, other human groups were already living there.

#### General Analysis

In this introduction to the Mutapa State, we have already learned the following information obtained from evidence (fossils are evidence that some living being was present in a location) on the African continent:
- The genus *Homo*, which encompasses different species, including our own, has been in Africa for millions of years (<htcite5>);
- The presence of *Homo* species in southern Africa also dates back thousands of years (<htcite6>);
- Bantu-speaking peoples, such as Shona speakers, have been in the region for thousands of years (<htcite7>).

Despite all this evidence, in <htdate3>, in Europe—thousands of kilometers away—it was established that not only Africa but the world would belong to peoples who had never lived in those places for long periods. This occurred through a document known as the Treaty of Tordesillas (<htcite8>), leading us to the following reflection:

source.text: Pakendorf, B., de Filippo, C., & Bostoen, K.

common.text:
Reflection 1: How so?

Imagine a place where it is impossible to prove that any of your ancestors ever lived—whether a recent or distant ancestor, over thousands of years.

Do you believe you have any right to that land?

What if someone who had also never been there wrote on a piece of paper that the place belongs to them? Would you feel comfortable going there and taking possession?

If you arrived and found people living there for thousands of years, what would you do?

The drafting of the Treaty of Tordesillas became problematic for the peoples who would be colonized—but not only for them. By imposing limits on the “division of the world” between just two kingdoms, the French king Francis I questioned it ironically, based on the worldview of the time: “The sun shines for me as for others. I would like to see the clause in Adam’s will that excludes me from the division of the world” (<htcite0>).

To conclude this introduction, it is important to note that the “division of the world” by Europeans did not imply immediate submission or acceptance by other peoples. In this text, we will explore the Mutapa State, which resisted colonialism until the 19th century, according to the Gregorian calendar (<htcite1>).

prerequisites: Before continuing with the text, we suggest studying the texts on the Bantu expansion already published on History Tracers (Bantu People; The Bantu Expansion).

history.text: Depending on where you live, you will most likely study only Egypt in Africa, a fact that can lead to mistaken conclusions regarding humans on the African continent. Therefore, before we proceed, we invite you to another reflection:

Reflection 2: Listing...

How many African empires, kingdoms, or states, besides Egypt, can you list between and ?

In your list, how many were you able to add that were created solely by Africans? Or were all of them made by Europeans?

Have you ever heard that much of Africa never had state-like organizations or civilization?

Due to Africa's territorial expanse and the focus of this text, we limit ourselves to answering the reflection by mentioning that, in Zimbabwe alone, after the fall of Great Zimbabwe, there were three other states (<htcite0>; <htcite1>; <htcite2>). In addition to these, another nineteen states can be found mentioned in the *World History Encyclopedia* (<htcite3>).

In this text, we will address the Mutapa dynasty, founder of the Mutapa State, around the end of the 15th century and the beginning of the 16th century, according to the Gregorian calendar.

source.page: Map of Antiquity and Medieval of Sub-Saharan African States

history.text: Around <htdate0>, Abraham brought new data that would inform the origin of the Mutapa kingdom's dynasty. According to him (<htcite0>):

• "NeMbire is said to have inhabited the north of the southern Zambezi plateau, in the first half of the 14th century, giving rise to a vast Karanga empire between the Zambezi and Limpopo rivers, which, it is presumed, developed into the Mutapa empire. NeMbire's daughter, Negupo, is said to have married Mutota Churuchamtuapa. Their daughter, Senwa or Nehanda, later had a son named Chikura Wadyambeu, by an unknown father. Chikura Wadyambeu was installed as the first 'Rozvi' ruler in Zimbabwe. Mutota Nyatsimba, who founded the Mutapa State in the north after succeeding Chikura in Zimbabwe in the early 15th century, was probably his son. This, in summary, was the history of the 'nuclear Shona' of the 14th century."

Before proceeding, we emphasize that the centuries mentioned in the previous text are based on the Gregorian calendar.

Despite the claim that the information was obtained from traditions dating back to the 14th century, all indications suggest that the reconstruction proposed by Abraham is, in fact, a tradition created in the 17th century (<htcite0>).

This initial account is not the only one associated with Mutota. Other traditions were also mentioned by Abraham, in which he apparently attempted to unite the Mutapa of the 15th century with the Budya of the 18th century and, finally, the Rozvi of the 17th century (<htcite1>), with no evidence for any of these connections.

Genealogy is not the only problem related to Mutota's ancestry. His very existence is not fully confirmed, although there is no doubt about the existence of the Mutapa kingdom. In addition to the lack of documentation, it should be noted that the first mention of his existence dates from a later period (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

The scenario surrounding Mutota becomes even more interesting with the possibility that he himself, if he existed, was not originally from the Zimbabwe region (<htcite4>; <htcite5>).

Reflection 3: When we do not question, we affirm...

The scenario of lack of documentation and late traditions presented by David N. Beach is not something new in humanity. Given this, reflect:
br />Can you identify another people who created characters in order to provide a basis for their own existence?

Did that other people also assume as historical truth something that would, in reality, be a poetic truth ()?

Suppose that, instead of the reflective works by David N. Beach that we are studying (; ), you had been presented with a work by Abraham with an apparently consistent narrative. Would you accept his proposal as true, or would you seek historical evidence to understand the motivations behind the narrative?

Part of this reflection is addressed by David N. Beach himself. Abraham wrote articles at a time when there was an idea among scholars of Africa, known as Africanists, that history could be extracted from a collection of traditions (<htcite0>). However, a more in-depth study shows that this attempt tends to stray from the facts.
As traditions indicate that Mutota had children (<htcite0>), this person must necessarily have existed.
According to ancient traditions (<htcite0>).

Like their supposed father, Matope is also considered a symbolic figure, which would make it impossible to associate the creation of the Mutapa State between <htdate0> and <htdate1> (<htcite0>; <htcite1>). Despite being a symbolic figure, it is precisely to him that the expansion of the Mutapa Kingdom is attributed, including the conquest as far as Ruenya, Mozambique (<htcite2>).

Due to the lack of evidence related to him, we will talk a little about personal relationships among the Shona. In general, the Shona population was monogamous, but their rulers had more than one wife, and among the principal wives were their sisters. Another fact to highlight is that wives were not considered property, but rather powerful personalities in their own right. One piece of evidence for this is the name given to the northern and eastern Shona dynasty in the 16th century, according to the Gregorian calendar: Karanga, which means “wife” (<htcite3>).

In addition to marriages, we would like to highlight that, according to the ancient narratives of Dionízio de Mello de Castro and Albino Manoel Pacheco, written in <htdate2> and <htdate3> respectively, no other Shona rulers would be known in a single period, until Abraham, in <htdate4>, proposed names through his research (<htcite4>).
As some traditions indicate that Mutota had children (<htcite0>), this person must necessarily have existed.
Based on narratives from <htdate0>, he begins to be mentioned as the son of Matope (<htcite0>).

In addition to not being reported in the early Portuguese publications about the State of Mutapa and Mozambique, the publications studied say little about this character, apparently created in <htdate0> during Abraham's studies (<htcite0>). What has been said about this king can also be applied to those who follow (Chikuyo, Chivere, Nogomo e Mavura) (<htcite1>).

Given this scenario of uncertainty related to these rulers, we will interrupt the specific study of these individuals, and we will begin to analyze the facts.

The first mention of Changamire occurs before <htdate0>, when a revolt against the Mutapa Kingdom took place. This revolt continued until <htdate1>, when the Mutapa regained control over the southern region where the Changamire were located, with evidence of ongoing wars until <htdate2> (<htcite0>).

Despite the failure of the first Changamire revolt, David N. Beach draws attention to confusions generated by this episode, which may have given rise to false accounts of marriages between the two most famous Shona states, Changamire and Mutapa (<htcite0>).

In addition to their initial revolt against the Mutapa, the Changamire also faced a revolt initiated by the Portuguese, who had an interest in their lands around <htdate0> (<htcite1>).

Despite these two defeats, this does not mean that the Changamire were completely dominated. There are records of the existence of this empire still in the 17th century (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

#### The Portuguese Arrival

The first contact between the Portuguese and the Mutapa kingdom occurred around <htdate0>, with the visit of Sancho de Toar, a fact that introduced the Mutapa to a new reality that was beginning to take shape in the Indian Ocean (<htcite0>).

At the beginning of the 16th century, according to the Gregorian calendar, the Mutapa kingdom did not have full control over access to the sea, as the Roro state controlled the mouth of the Zambezi River in Mozambique. Given this scenario, the Mutapa who contacted the Portuguese at the port of Sofala, also in Mozambique, informed them that the region's gold was primarily controlled by them.

This communication continued to evolve during the first half of the 16th century, to the point where the Mutapa requested Portugal to send an “ambassador,” who was recognized by the Mutapa rulers as the “great wife” of the Mutapa. Despite the honors, they were treated in an inferior position within the kingdom (<htcite1>).

#### Invasion Plans

It is not possible to rule out that the Portuguese may have envisioned the possibility of invading the Mutapa state, especially after the murder of Friar Gonçalo da Silveira by the Mutapa empire in <htdate1>, after the friar went about converting people wherever he passed. However, although we cannot assert that they attempted to conquer the territory, we can say that the Mutapa state remained intact.

The Portuguese were directed to a region where there were no Shona people, and an old Mutapa-Portuguese treaty was altered in favor of the Mutapa (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

Reflection 4: Religion and State

The information associated with Friar Gonçalo da Silveira does not allow us to assert misconduct on the part of the religious figure ().

On the other hand, we cannot rule out that religion was used by empires, such as the Portuguese, to control the population ().

Still regarding the religious scenario, A. Nicolaides reports that Muslims were already conducting trade with Mutapa and were responsible for warning the emperor that the Jesuit priest was a Portuguese instrument to dominate the empire ().

Should people use beliefs to dominate others?

Both Muslims and Portuguese Christians had religious leaders among their men. Were these leaders merely aligned with their beliefs, or did they know exactly their importance for the interests of others?

When different people have different interpretations of something, such as beliefs, would it not be better to study and learn from the other, rather than using it to dominate someone?
Two of his sons became rulers of Mutapa, therefore he existed (<htcite0>).
It is not possible to assert that the two sons mentioned below are from the same mother, but their existence is impossible without her/them.
According to the source consulted (<htcite0>).

According to the source consulted (<htcite0>). We note that, as the source only mentions “uncle,” it is not possible to categorically state that he is Gatsi Rusere's brother. Despite this, we still include him here, as it is the most common scenario.

Gatsi Rusere is the first ruler we can consider historically confirmed who will be discussed in this text. He began his rule in <htdate0>, inaugurating a period with various revolts (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

The relationship between Gatsi Rusere and the Portuguese was different from that of his predecessors, because, due to turmoil, he had to go into exile, but eventually returned in <htdate1>. He requested and received aid from the Portuguese in order to retake his kingdom (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

The good relationship between the ruler and the Portuguese kingdom, however, was short-lived. Despite his intentions to allow the Portuguese to exploit silver mines, they began to instigate internal revolts in the government, such as in Chikova, Zimbabwe, in addition to, for a long period, not paying the *curva*—gifts equivalent to the value of 3,000 cruzados— to be able to exploit and carry out trade in Mozambique (<htcite4>).

Reflection 5: The owner paying tribute?

According to the vision of the Treaty of Tordesillas, Portugal was the “owner” of the Mutapa empire ().

Why did the “owners” pay tribute to exploit what would be theirs?

Was the idea of possession, established by the Treaty of Tordesillas, distorted?
As his son assumed government in <htdate0>, his existence is proven (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).
According to the analyzed works (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

Nyambu Kapararidze had a long civil war with his uncle, which lasted until the year <htdate0>, when his uncle passed away (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

The clashes with his uncle were not easy, as the latter had the support of the Portuguese and maintained a great alliance with the Dominicans. Nyambu Kapararidze even tried to ask the Portuguese for help and establish trade relations with them, but he was ignored. He had to go into exile abroad, remaining a constant threat to his uncle (<htcite2>).

Upon the death of his brother, or brother-in-law, Mavura Mhande, who had good relations with the Portuguese, allied himself with the Dominicans and, with their help, declared himself a rival to the Mutapa. As a consequence, there were years of civil war (<htcite0>). This leads us to reflect on a possible global strategy of the Portuguese:

Reflection 6: Divide and conquer

When analyzing letters from Saint José de Anchieta, written in , we see that in one of them there is an account about an indigenous person ():

"Our principal, great friend and protector Martim Afonso, also died, who, after having made himself an enemy of his own brothers and relatives, for the love of God and His Church, and after Our Lord had given him victory over his enemies..."

Could the Portuguese, with the help of religious organizations, have implemented a policy of dividing families to facilitate the conquest of territories, both in America (Abya Yala) and in Africa?

Were the Jesuits in Brazil (Pindorama) and the Dominicans in Mutapa (Zimbabwe) directly or indirectly linked to local conflicts? Wars are the opposite of love. Is it considered that these religious figures might have gone against one of the commandments they were supposed to follow, which says: "You must love your neighbour as yourself" ()?

With the rise of Mavura, the Muslims were completely expelled from the Mutapa territory, and the Dominicans had complete freedom to carry out their conversion work. Another direct impact was the end of the payment, by the Portuguese, of tribute to the Mutapa Empire. But, without a doubt, the main consequence was the submission of the Mutapa to the Portuguese throne, despite them still remaining as local rulers. This led the Portuguese to begin a monumental extraction of gold in the region, which even generated conflicts among the Portuguese themselves in Mutapa lands (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).
As the ruler had a son (<htcite0>), his existence is proven.
According to the source studied (<htcite0>)

exercise_v2.question: Tanto os ancestrais de Mutota, assim como ele, são pessoas históricas?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Apesar das narrativas antigas, criadas 3 séculos depois de sua hipotética existência, não é possível afirmar categoricamente que ele mesmo tenha existido.

exercise_v2.question: Depois de Mutota, todos os demais reis são históricos?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Mutota, e seu hipotético filho Matope, são personagens que possuem mais informações associadas a eles. Os reis que vem na sequência foram pouco explorados nos trabalhos estudados, entre outros motivos, pela dúvida que realmente existiram.

exercise_v2.question: O tratado de Tordesilhas foi respeitado em todos os locais do mundo?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Além da França o ter questionado, ao estudarmos o povo Shona, é possível observar que os estados Mutapa e Changamire continuaram existindo e possuindo seus territórios, independente de uma interpretação muito específica ocorrendo em partes da Europa sobre o mundo.
